“It’s really difficult representing football on stage,” says Graham, a passionate football lover who grew up supporting Rangers and dreaming of a professional playing career. “People who know the sport will see through it if it’s not right. I’ve not often seen it done well.”

This is the first time he has tackled the problem: Stefano Massini’s story of World War One ammunition factory workers, kicking a ball during their shift break and going on to establish one of the most popular football teams in the country, overcame his doubts.

“My youngest daughter has played football from the age of nine and it was there in front of me on the pitch: the girls’ reticence to be seen, their fear of taking up space, of risking potential humiliation or letting people down. You could see them physically shrinking. It was heartbreaking.”

In the seven years he has watched his daughter and her friends play, he has witnessed a metamorphosis. “They grew in physical confidence. They started to literally take up space. And I thought, that’s a metaphor. That’s political.”

For Newman, it represented the key to The Ladies Football Club script, adapted by Tim Firth from Massini’s original monologue. “A big reason for wanting to tell this particular story is because it’s about injustice,” she says. “It’s about the fact the FA banned women’s football. And in this moment, the rights we have won as women are, in many parts of the world, being taken away again.”

Newman played football as a child growing up in Croydon – “It was what you did, you saw a ball, you kicked it. Uuntil you got older and someone told you that wasn’t for you.”

In her production, the choreography becomes more expansive as the players grow in skill, the ensemble lifting and spinning each other as they execute a scissor kick or fly through the air to save a goal. The fictional team’s journey has been mirrored by the cast. While some were already football nuts – Cara Theobold, who plays the central role of Violet, runs her own community team – others have had to face their own insecurities about their physicality. “You can see how far they’ve come,” says Graham, proudly. “Their legs are rooted, their hips are fluid, their head comes forward to the ball.”

It is ironic that theatre has often felt so distanced from football and sport more generally, given all they have in common: live, embodied drama; an immersive shared experience that can only be truly appreciated in the moment. Sporting stadiums are, essentially, theatre in the round. Yet when Graham was at university, in the drama society and the first XI, he felt uncomfortable enough about his opposing passions that “I didn’t tell either of those parties that I was in the other”.

Today, the cultural climate has shifted significantly. Sport’s planetary takeover means it is now a near-universal touchstone and its role in contemporary society and politics makes it increasingly attractive as a theatrical subject. As storytellers increasingly recognise its value – and its potential audience draw – the division between sport and the loftier performing arts seems to be dissolving.

Last year, the RSC set Much Ado About Nothing in Serie A, an Edinburgh fringe show retold Lady Macbeth’s story on the netball court and a new musical launched about the first all-female sailing crew in the Whitbread Round the World race.

This isn’t just about the jocks and the nerds finally speaking each other’s language, however. Newman has worked on football plays before – including one about Bolton’s 1923 FA Cup victory, And Did Those Feet – and what interests her about sport is the example of people working together towards a common (and sometimes actual) goal. “It feels really important, for now, to show people being collegiate. There’s a strong sense of that across women’s sport.”

Three miles from the Crucible, the financial collapse of Sheffield Wednesday continues to play out its own saga. Football lovers of all stripes in the city can appreciate just how swiftly the collectivist roots of their sport are being dismantled by privatisation and profiteering. For Hillsborough faithfuls, looking for some light in the dark times, perhaps The Ladies Football Club can provide it.

Football.title: ITV in talks with advertisers over showing commercials during World Cup drinks breaks
Football.content:
ITV is in talks with its commercial partners about showing adverts during the mid-half drinks stoppages that will take place in every match at this summer’s World Cup.

Global broadcasters have been briefed on Fifa’s stipulations for the three-minute hydration breaks, which will take place after 22 minutes of each half irrespective of the temperature.

A two-minutes-and-10-second commercial break will be permitted if TV companies opt to cut away from the on-field action although they can also choose to stay with the live pictures or adopt a hybrid approach using a split screen.

ITV may resist the temptation to sell full commercial breaks in favour of continuing the so-called “pic-in-pic advertising” it utilised for the first time during this year’s Six Nations Championship.

ITV declined to comment, but sources at the commercial broadcaster indicated that its use of in‑picture adverts whilst scrums are set in their Six Nations coverage had been well received by viewers, who they are wary of antagonising, particularly during such an important event as the World Cup.

In-picture adverts are seen as less disruptive and enable commentary teams to continue their analysis of the match, and pick up details of any tactical instructions relayed by coaching staff to players during the break.

ITV has joint live rights for the World Cup in the UK with the BBC, for whom adverts are not an issue, other than promoting its own programming.

The World Cup will be the first major tournament to stop all matches midway through each half for three minutes, although hydration breaks were used at last summer’s Club World Cup when the in-stadium temperature exceeded 32C (90F).

Fifa announced in December that it would introduce the break in each of the 104 World Cup matches as a “player welfare” measure, but it will also bring significant commercial benefits, particularly in the long term.

Football.title: Tottenham to stick with manager Igor Tudor for now, but Spurs legend keen to step in
Football.content:
Tottenham to stick with manager Igor Tudor for now, but Spurs legend keen to step in

Igor Tudor will continue as Tottenham manager for the immediate future, talkSPORT understands.

Some Spurs fans are calling for Croatian Tudor to be sacked after losing all three of his games in charge, plunging them deeper into relegation danger.

3 Tudor has only pushed Tottenham closer to relegation since taking charge Credit: Getty

While Spurs are reluctant to sack a manger for the second time this season the pressure will continue to grow on Tudor if they lose their upcoming Champions League last 16 first leg tie at Atletico Madrid on Tuesday and next Sunday's Premier League trip to Liverpool.



If Spurs did decide Tudor is not the right man then former striker Robbie Keane, currently in charge for Hungarian champion Ferencvaros, is believed to be keen on the job.

Former Spurs Mauricio Pochettino remains a long-term option but will not be available until after leading the United States of America at the upcoming World Cup.

Spurs also have historic interest in Fulham's Marco Silva while Dougie Freedman being a leading contender to become their new sporting director raises the prospect of a move for Crystal Palace's Oliver Glasner, assuming they stay up.

Jamie O'Hara at his ANGRIEST as he STORMS OUT OF STUDIO after Tottenham EMBARRASSMENT!

Tudor's tumultuous tenure

Tudor was brought on board in February to replace Thomas Frank for the remainder of the season just days after the Dane was axed.



A 2-1 loss at home to Newcastle proved to be the final nail in the coffin for Frank, who was appointed in the summer on a three-year contract.

Frank was Tottenham's fifth permanent manager since Mauricio Pochettino departed in 2019.



Despite having been brought on with the hope he'd provide an instant boost to a stagnating squad, Tudor has thus far failed to turn around Tottenham's fortunes.

Instead, they have slid closer towards the relegation zone and are still searching for a first league win since December 28, when they won away at Crystal Palace.



With just nine games remaining in the league this season, time is running out for Tudor to turn around the ship.

3 Tudor has lost every match in his interim spell at Tottenham Credit: Getty

He'd be Keane

Should Tottenham go for Keane, a stop in north London would mark the fourth job in his managerial career.

He spent three games as Indian side ATK's player-manager before he got his first proper role in June 2023, when he took over Maccabi Tel Aviv.

Keane proved to be a hit in Israel, as he led the club to an Israeli Premier League title and the Israel Toto Cup in his only full season at the club.



The former Ireland international was appointed Ferencvaros boss in January 2025 and didn't take long to impress, as he helped the club win the Hungarian top flight for the 2024/25 season.

Keane's impressive work on the continent has drawn admirers in the Premier League as he emerged as a contender to replace Glasner at Crystal Palace.

The 45-year-old was also discussed as a potential interim option following the sacking of Frank, but it did not eventuate.

3 Spurs are in seriously deep trouble just one point above the drop zone

Should Keane be handed the reins in north London, it would mark a sensational return to the club he enjoyed individual success as a player.



The tricky forward scored an impressive 122 goals in 306 games across all competitions in two separate spells.

He was also a member of the Tottenham team that won the League Cup in 2008, which remained the club's last piece of silverware until Ange Postecoglou helped the club win the Europa League last season to end a 17-year trophy drought.

Football.title: Premier League star names one away stadium that delivers ‘special’ atmosphere
Football.content:
Elland Road is the only Premier League stadium to match the Stadium of Light's electric atmosphere this season.

That is the view of Sunderland's Luke O'Nien, who was left impressed by Leeds' vocal home support in his side's 1-0 victory on Tuesday night.

4 O'Nien completed his first full 90 minutes in the Premier League at Elland Road Credit: Getty

Making his first-ever top-flight start, O'Nien enjoyed a memorable evening as Habib Diarra's 70th minute penalty secured the Black Cats a battling victory on the road.



After leading the team out as captain, the 31-year-old kept a clean sheet and was later named player of the match for his performance.

It marked the latest high in O'Nien's incredible career, which has seen him climb from non league to the Premier League.

After loan spells with Wealdstone in the sixth and seventh tiers, the defender impressed at Wycombe, earning him a move to Sunderland in 2018, who were then in League One.

O'Nien has since established himself as a hero on Wearside, playing a key role in two promotions to take the club back to the top.



Elite Elland Road

Tipped to struggle at the start of the season, Sunderland have impressed in their first Premier League campaign since 2017, currently sitting 11th and in the mix for European football.

Their electric home atmospheres at the Stadium of Light have also earned praise, something O'Nien was quick to stress on talkSPORT.



Joining Friday's Breakfast Show, the Black Cats star insisted the vocal support has been a constant during his time at the club, and claimed few Premier League grounds manage to live up to this.

That is, apart from Elland Road, with O'Nien hailing the Leeds atmosphere for the midweek clash as 'special'.



Discussing Sunderland's following this season, he said: "It's packed out [the Stadium of Light], even every away end.

4 Sunderland unveiled an incredible tifo ahead of their Tyne-Wear derby win over Newcastle in December Credit: Getty

4 36,713 fans created a buzzing atmosphere at Elland Road as Leeds hosted the Black Cats Credit: Getty

“The Leeds atmosphere the other day was special because, I think that the Premier League's been incredible, but the atmosphere hasn't quite matched the level, I'd like to say.

“When you go to a place like Leeds - the game, what it is, it's special. And our fans have been like that since League One."



With the 49,000-capacity Stadium of Light filled to the brim each week in the top-flight, O'Nien reminded listeners that Sunderland fans also turned out in their numbers throughout the divisions.

He continued: “We had 35,000 fans in League One and we're a sell-out in the Premier League.

"So it's special that the fans have always believed in us. They've kept supporting us and I think we're returning that.

“We still have a lot to do, but I think we're returning that, them just staying with us, now with some good performances this season.”

4 O'Nien has spent over a decade at Sunderland, and played 44 games in his side's promotion-winning campaign last season Credit: Getty



What next for Sunderland?

It's fair to say Sunderland have surprised many with their strong showings in the Premier League this term.

With newly-promoted sides routinely struggling to establish themselves in the top-flight, the Black Cats have avoided a relegation battle, and hit the magic 40-point mark with victory at Leeds.

And Regis Le Bris' side may have even more to celebrate at the end of the season, with European football still a genuine possiblity.

With seventh-place believed to be the cut-off point for Conference League qualification, Sunderland are currently just four points behind Brentford, who occupy the position.

Not since 1973 have the Wearsiders qualified for Europe, which came after FA Cup glory in the previous season - their last major trophy.



And Sunderland may also have one eye on rewriting history here, as they take on Port Vale in their next match, the fifth round of the competition on Sunday.

Football.title: Robert Lewandowski puts asterisk on Harry Kane goals with England star eyeing his Bundesliga record
Football.content:
Robert Lewandowski puts asterisk on Harry Kane goals with England star eyeing his Bundesliga record

Robert Lewandowski is keeping a watchful eye on the Bundesliga Golden Boot race this season.

But he's not doing it to admire Harry Kane's ridiculous form in front of goal for Bayern Munich.

3 Kane has Bundesliga history in his sights Credit: Getty

Instead, Lewandowski is waiting to see whether Kane will break the record for most goals scored in a Bundesliga season.



How many goals did Lewandowski score in his record-breaking season?

Lewandowski set the record during the 2020/21 campaign when he slammed home an absurd 41 goals in just 29 games.

At present, Kane has 30 goals from 24 games.

It means Kane must net 12 more times in Bayern's ten remaining Bundesliga fixtures if he is to eclipse Lewandowski's absurd record.

Yet Kane might have needed to score even more goals had Lewandowski completed the entirety of his record-breaking term.

"Harry Kane is scoring always so many goals and he's playing really good and he's doing a great job, and then I saw I did this in 29 games," Lewandowski told Sky Sports.



"If I played 34 games, I can only imagine I could even score more!

"Because of him, I can be more proud of my record now!"

Lewandowski's record-setting campaign was just of several seasons in which he terrorised rival defenders in the German top flight.

How many Bundesliga goals did Lewandowski score?

In fact, the Pole won the Bundesliga Golden Boot award seven times.

3 Lewandowski scored a total of 344 goals for Bayern Credit: Getty - Contributor



Robert Lewandowski's Bundesliga goal tally 2010/11 at Borussia Dortmund: 33 games, 8 goals 2011/12 at Borussia Dortmund: 34 games, 22 goals 2012/13 at Borussia Dortmund: 31 games, 24 goals 2013/14 at Borussia Dortmund: 33 games, 20 goals 2014/15 at Bayern Munich: 31 games, 17 goals 2015/16 at Bayern Munich: 32 games, 30 goals 2016/17 at Bayern Munich: 33 games, 30 goals 2017/18 at Bayern Munich: 30 games, 29 goals 2018/19 at Bayern Munich: 33 games, 22 goals 2019/20 at Bayern Munich: 31 games, 34 goals 2020/21 at Bayern Munich: 29 games, 41 goals 2021/22 at Bayern Munich: 34 games, 35 goals Total: 384 games, 312 goals

Adding further to his legend, Lewandowski accomplished the feat in five consecutive seasons, beginning with the 2017/18 campaign and ending in 2021/22, which was his last term in the Bundesliga before he joined Barcelona.

At the time of Lewandowski's departure, he had scored an absurd 312 goals in 384 Bundesliga fixtures.



The tally has Lewandowski sitting as the all-time non-German goalscorer in Bundesliga history, but he remains 54 goals shy of Gerd Muller's all-time record.

How far away is Kane from Lewandowski's records?

Unfortunately for Kane, he has a serious mountain to climb if he is to even come close to Lewandowski's goalscoring feats.

To date, the England international has an impressive 92 goals in 87 Bundesliga fixtures.

However, Kane turns 33 in July, so time is not exactly on his side.

3 Kane has scored goals for fun ever since he joined Bayern Credit: AFP

But even if Kane doesn't surpass Lewandowski's record for goals scored in a single Bundesliga season, he is poised to win the competition's Golden Boot for a third-straight year.



The ex-Tottenham superstar's tally of 30 goals is 16 clear of Stuttgart's Deniz Undav, who is second in goalscoring standings.

Kane will look to inch closer to Lewandowski's record on Friday as table-topping Bayern host Borussia Monchengladbach.

Football.title: Fans all think Tottenham are relegated and ‘Sir’ Jason Cundy is leading the laughter at the chaos
Football.content:
Fans all think Tottenham are relegated and ‘Sir’ Jason Cundy is leading the laughter at the chaos

It's official: The majority of football fans believe Tottenham will be relegated this season.

Oh, and they will be ready to point and laugh at the north London outfit's demise.

5 It has been a season to forget for the Tottenham faithful Credit: Getty

In a poll conducted on talkSPORT.com, fans were given the opportunity to have their say on whether they believed Tottenham would go down.



The first option read: "Yes, and we'll laugh."

Those who were a little more optimistic could click on: "No, there are worse teams than Spurs."

The verdict is in...

Unfortunately for Tottenham, more than 60 per cent of voters believed the club will be in the Championship next season.

As it stands, Igor Tudor's side sit 16th in the table with 29 points, one clear of 18th-placed West Ham United.

However, Tottenham's form makes for alarming viewing and the workplace a hard place to be - just ask talkSPORT's resident Tottenham fan, Jamie O'Hara who was pushed to his limit on his radio show, the Sports Bar.



Horrendous form backs up football fans' verdict

No Premier League team has taken fewer points from their last six games, with Tottenham collecting just one point.

That solitary point came from a 2-2 draw against Manchester City on February 1, whilst their last league win was against Crystal Palace on 28 December.



Their last league win came on 28 December, against Palace, while they have not won at home in the league since the start of December.

Since then the club have actually sacked more managers than they’ve won games in 2026 given the fact they parted ways with Thomas Frank, who they appointed last summer on a three-year contract, and replaced him with Igor Tudor on a deal until the end of the season.



5 Tottenham would be by far the biggest club to have been relegated from the Premier League Credit: Getty

5 O'Hara couldn't take it anymore as Cundy sat in a clown suit and Ange Postecoglu mask

Three managers, including Frank, have come and gone with Ruben Amorim and Enzo Maresca losing their jobs, too in that time.

Arsenal, meanwhile are on course to win the quadruple and have scored 44 goals in all competitions compared to the 18 scored by Spurs since that last league win.

Even Domino's pizza got a dig in, claiming to have delivered 89 million pizzas in the UK since 28 December. Chelsea fan and Sports Bar host Jason Cundy has had particular fun as he wound up co-host O'Hara to the point that he had to step away from the microphone.



It prompted another Blue in talkSPORT host Rory Jennings to suggest his services to broadcasting deserves a knighthood.

The latest demoralising result has ignited calls for Tottenham to move on from Tudor, with O'Hara wanting former Spurs boss Harry Redknapp to make a sensational return.

Although Redknapp would be open to the opportunity, it's not one he expects to happen.

Whether it's Tudor, Redknapp or someone else, keeping Tottenham in the Premier League will be no easy feat.

5 talkSPORT host Rory Jennings loved watching Jason Cundy speak for the nation as he revelled in Tottenham's demise



5 Tudor has only pushed Tottenham closer to relegation since taking charge Credit: Getty

A tough run home

Three of Tottenham's opponents in their final nine league fixtures are in the top six.

The run home also includes matches against fellow survival hopefuls Nottingham Forest and Leeds United.

Tottenham also take on Wolves, who beat both Aston Villa and Liverpool in the space of a week.

Spurs round out the season on May 24 with a home fixture against Everton, but should results go against them, they could enter the match with their fate already sealed.



Should that transpire, you best believe rival fans will be ready and waiting to take the mick.

Football.title: talkSPORT BET has paid out on Tottenham to be relegated after fifth consecutive defeat
Football.content:
talkSPORT BET has paid out on Tottenham to be relegated after fifth consecutive defeat

talkSPORT BET has already PAID OUT on Tottenham to be relegated on all single bets placed before 6 March.

The damaging home defeat against Crystal Palace left Spurs sitting just one point above the Premier League relegation zone with only nine matches of the season remaining.

2 Spurs are in seriously deep trouble just one point above the drop zone

The 3-1 defeat at home against the Eagles was the club's fifth consecutive defeat and leaves them without a league victory in 11 matches dating back to their final game of 2025, a winless streak five games longer than the next closest team.

Thousands of Spurs fans were seen leaving the stadium at half-time, when their side were losing 3-1 down and a man down.

The London derby was seen as the real test to determine how likely the club's relegation threat really was, as they came up against a Crystal Palace side who had won just two of their last 13 matches, but didn't appear to put up too much of a fight against their south London opposition.

The result sparked outrage among Spurs fans that the once unthinkable relegation may actually be a very real possibility, with the likes of former Tottenham midfielder and avid fan Jamie O'Hara storming out of the talkSPORT studio live on air with co-host Jason Cundy donning an Ange Postecoglou mask and a clown suit.

It has been a season to forget having began with a new manager, with Thomas Frank replacing Postecoglu, who guided the club to Europa League success.

When Frank, who had led the Bees to top 10 Premier League finishes in two his last three seasons with the club, took over in the summer, the side were priced at 50/1 for relegation.

After a strong start to life - Spurs were third in the Premier League in November and were unbeaten at their new stadium at the time - Spurs were priced as high as 250/1 to go down, which a couple of talkSPORT BET punters even got behind.

Since then, Tottenham have played 20 league games and picked up a mere 12 points, with only Burnley collecting fewer in that time span.

Frank was eventually sacked as Tottenham boss after a 2-1 home defeat against Newcastle which left the side hovering just five points above the relegation zone. The price for relegation was bludgeoned down to 6/1.

2 talkSPORT Bet has paid out on Spurs to be relegated already

Former Juventus manager Igor Tudor has since come in and lost all three games in charge, kicking off with a 4-1 loss at home against north London rivals Arsenal, before defeats in the last week against both Fulham and Crystal Palace, their odds for relegation now as short as 13/8.

Football.title: Aston Villa boss Unai Emery branded the Premier League’s most overrated manager
Football.content:
Unai Emery is not a top manager and he has been 'getting away with it' during Aston Villa's poor run of form.

That's the view of talkSPORT's Angelina Kelly, who has branded Emery the Premier League's most overrated coach in a brutal assessment of the Spaniard's managerial career.

4 Unai Emery has never finished lower than seventh in the Premier League with Aston Vilal Credit: Getty

Having been in the title race just weeks ago, a nightmare run of form and injuries to key players now sees Villa fighting for a top four finish.

On Wednesday night, Emery's side were thumped 4-1 at home by rivals Chelsea, slumping to back-to-back defeats after a 2-0 loss to bottom club Wolves.

The damaging results mean Villa have won just once in their last six league games, who are now just three points clear of Liverpool and Chelsea in fifth and sixth place respectively.

With fifth likely to be the cut-off point for Champions League football, there are now concerns Villa could fail to qualify.

Kelly: Emery on 'too high of a pedestal'

Villa's poor form caught the eye of talkSPORT's Kelly, who used it to support her football hot take on the latest episode of Fiery Fridays.

Joining Alex Crook and Adrian Durham on the talkSPORT podcast, the trio were asked for their most controversial football opinions, and Emery soon found himself in the firing line.

Giving her hot take, Kelly began: “Unai Emery is the most overrated manager currently in the Premier League."

Since taking charge at Villa Park in 2022, Emery has never finished lower than seventh, as well as making the top four twice.

This has earned the Spanish coach huge praise, and has seen him linked with a number of big name jobs, namely the Manchester United vacancy, but Red Devils fan Kelly wasn't convinced by his credentials.

She continued: “Unai Emery is not a bad manager. He's actually a good one, but for me, he's put on much too high of a pedestal.

4 Aston Villa are fighting to secure Champions League football for the third time under Emery Credit: Getty

“I think up until now, he's done well overall with Aston Villa, getting them consistently into different levels of European football, finishing no lower than seventh, which I do applaud.

“However, the fact that he's been hailed as a man who would be worthy of one of the top jobs - he was linked to Manchester United not too long ago, I'm just not having it."

'Everyone gives him a free pass'

Emery previously spent two years in charge of Paris Saint-Germain, winning Ligue 1 in 2018, before being appointed Arsenal boss.

However, his spell in north London didn't go according to plan, as he was sacked after just 18 months in the role with the Gunners enduring their worst run of form since 1992.

Kelly said: “I think when he was at Arsenal, who have far higher aims and expectations than Villa - no disrespect to them - he fumbled it, was sacked after their worst run of form for 27 years in 2019.

“His successor [Mikel Arteta] is doing a much better job.”

4 Emery was sacked by Arsenal in 2019 after going seven games without a win Credit: Getty Images

Continuing to question Emery's credentials, she went on: “His record domestically isn’t amazing - apart from PSG, who let’s face it, are richer than the rest of the French league combined.

“He’s never won a domestic trophy, never really been in a title race outside of France, because I don't think this season counts.

“I know that they’ve had injuries recently [Villa]. But earlier this season everyone thought he was a genius, they’d won 10 games in a row, were apparently in this title race.

“Recently they've dropped off, nobody's had anything to say about it, he's almost getting away with it.

“Villa have won once in their last seven in all competitions - a last minute win against Brighton, lost to bottom club Wolves, destroyed at home by Chelsea, couldn't beat Leeds, [they lost to] 10-man Brentford at home.

“Everyone gives him a free pass, but not here on Fiery Fridays!"

4 Emery won the Europa League title for three straight seasons as Sevilla manager Credit: GETTY

Emery's biggest managerial achievement is his stunning Europa League record, winning the competition a record four times - three times with Sevilla, and once with Villareal in 2021.

This didn't move Kelly from her stance though, who said: “It’s great that he won them, but it’s no indication for me that he’s an elite-level manager.”

Durham's defence

Adrian Durham was quick to defend Emery, insisting that his spell at Arsenal was far from the failure it has been made out to be.

With the Gunners finishing eighth in Arteta's first full season in charge, he stressed that Emery had been tasked with a diffiuclt job during a period of transition at the Emirates.

The talkSPORT presenter argued: “Saying that this guy now [Arteta] is making Unai Emery look bad is a massive rewriting of history."

Despite this, Durham agreed with Kelly's assessment that Emery is just short of being considered a world class manager.

He explained: “Now where I agree with you, Angelina, is that I've heard people say: ‘I love that Unai Emery. He really knows what [he's doing]. He knows this, he knows that’.

“I don't see the evidence that he's actually elite.”

Football.title: Harry Redknapp directly responds to calls for stunning Tottenham return
Football.content:
Much like the bat signal is beamed into Gotham City's night sky, a silhouette of Harry Redknapp's face could soon be lit above the Tottenham Hotspur Stadium.

The calls for Redknapp to make a sensational return to north London have grown louder after Tottenham interim boss Igor Tudor oversaw a third defeat in as many games on Thursday night.

4 Interim head coach Igor Tudor has quickly realised the magnitude of his task Credit: Getty

A 3-1 loss at home to Crystal Palace consigned Tottenham to their fifth-straight league defeat and left them one point above 18th-placed West Ham United.



With Tudor failing to generate a spark since he took over from Thomas Frank, who was sacked just eight months into a three-year contract, there are calls for Tottenham to once again make a change in the dugout as they look to avoid the unthinkable fate of relegation.

Redknapp's name is one of several that has been bandied about as a potential replacement for Tudor, should Tottenham pull the trigger once more.

The 79-year-old has not held a managerial role since September 2017, yet should the call come, he'd be willing to dust off the suit for one last job.

But it's not a scenario he can see happening.

Redknapp's return?

"Of course," Redknapp told Ally McCoist and Ray Parlour on talkSPORT Breakfast.



"I'd have to, wouldn't I?

"But it's not going to happen. I can't see it happening. I'll be at Cheltenham on Wednesday, Thursday and Friday."

One prominent pundit who wants Redknapp back at the club is former Spurs midfielder and Sports Bar co-host Jamie O'Hara.



Speaking on Thursday's edition of the Sports Bar, which included a moment where he stormed out of the studio following Jason Cundy's wind-up antics, O'Hara anointed Redknapp as the man to steer Tottenham to safety.

4 Spurs are in seriously deep trouble just one point above the drop zone



Harry Redknapp's time in charge of Tottenham Took over at Tottenham on October 26 in 2008 Departed Tottenham on June 13 in 2012 Managed 198 games and finished with a record of 98 wins, 50 draws and 50 defeats across all competitions Led Tottenham to league finishes of 8th (08/09), 4th (09/10), 5th (10/11) and 4th (11/12) Named Premier League manager of the season in 2009/10 Led Tottenham to its highest finish in the Premier League era (4th) during the 2009/10 season, although it was bettered when they came 2nd in the 2016/17 season Tottenham's fourth-place finish in 2009/10 season helped the club qualify for the Champions League for the first time in their history

O'Hara leads calls for Redknapp

"You need someone at that football club who cares about, it, who has an affiliation to it, who is going to get the fans onside," O'Hara said.

"I reckon Harry Redknapp should get a phone call tonight (Thursday).

"They should say, 'Listen, you've got to come back, we need you back.'

"Bring in some people who know the football club. Bring some people that are going to have some respect from the players and just play a formation where the players know what they're doing.

"You can't just come in and change a formation and play players out of position when you're fighting a relegation battle."

Given O'Hara's unwavering passion for Tottenham, Redknapp was asked by McCoist if he'd bring on the ex-midfielder to his coaching staff should he end up returning to the club.



4 It has been well over a decade since Redknapp was last in charge of Tottenham Credit: AFP

Although Redknapp did not directly respond to the question and admitted he missed O'Hara's on-air meltdown, he replied: "He's (O'Hara) a good lad, what a lad he is."

Aside from Redknapp, a former Tottenham manager who some may call for a sensational return is Tim Sherwood.

Sherwood needs to see more 'hurt'

Sherwood, who was a member of Redknapp's first team coaching staff, took over from Andre Villas Boas in December 2013.

It ultimately proved to be a short-term reign as he was sacked just five months later, with his entire stint lasting only 28 games in total.



Although Sherwood did not address a potential return during his appearance on talkSPORT Breakfast, there was one issue he was especially critical of.

"At the moment, when I look at it, it doesn't look like they've got the hurt," Sherwood said.

4 Tottenham is just one of two clubs Sherwood has managed during his coaching career Credit: Getty Images - Getty

"Where is the deterrent to get relegated? I'm not sure it's there.

"Well it certainly isn't going to hurt them as much as it's going to hurt the fans, and that's what it's all about."

Tottenham will look to put their domestic nightmares behind them next Tuesday when they travel to Spain for the first leg of their Champions League round of 16 clash against Atletico Madrid.
